data.title: AppLovin: Overly Fast And Furious Rally - Near-Term Performance Likely Lumpy
data.description:
APP's Success Story Deserves To Be Awarded, Just Not This Much

We previously covered AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP) in December 2024, discussing how its promising e-commerce advertising results had driven its stock price up by +718% on a YTD basis, well outperforming the wider market and its ad-tech peers.

Even so, with the stock increasingly expensive while being buoyed by momentum, we had believed there was no margin of safety at those inflated levels, since FY2025 was also likely to suffer from tougher YoY comparisons.

APP 1Y Stock Price

Since then, APP has proven us wrong again by charting an impressive stock price return of +44.6%, well outperforming the wider market at +0.8%, thanks to the double beat FQ4'24 performance and the promising FQ1'25 guidance.

For reference, the ad-tech company reported FQ4'24 revenues of $1.37B (+14.1% QoQ/+44.2% YoY), adj EBITDA margins of 61.7% (+1.7 points QoQ/+11.8 YoY), and adj EPS of $1.73 (+38.4% QoQ/+253% YoY).

Most importantly, APP's high-growth advertising revenues of $999.48M (+19.6% QoQ/+73.3% YoY) and extremely rich adj EBITDA margins of 77.7% (-0.5 points QoQ/+4.9 YoY) compared to that of the Apps segment at $373.29M (+2.8% QoQ/-0.9% YoY) and 19.1% (+0.7 points QoQ/+4.2 YoY), respectively, underscore why the pivot to the e-commerce advertising market has been a great move after all.

This also explains why the management may have chosen to divest its Apps business, given the slowing topline growth and the diluting impact on its bottom-lines.

If anything, APP has gone one step further to feed the exuberance by offering a robust FQ1'25 advertising revenue guidance of $1.04B (+4% QoQ/+55.2% YoY) and adj EBITDA margin of 78.3% (+0.6 points QoQ/+5.8 YoY), well exceeding the consensus estimates, with it underscoring why the stock has outperformed as it has on a one-day basis.

Given the promising early results, we can understand why the management has sought to diversify its advertising verticals beyond gaming "to serve the entire global advertising economy," significantly aided by the "demand from advertisers wanting to join our platform is high."

APP's nascent advertising opportunity is why it has been able to report accelerated growth against the global digital advertising market growth at +10.9% to $667.58B in 2024, with it underscoring its intermediate term high-growth profile.

The same has been observed in the ad-tech company's extremely promising "$3 million in run rate adjusted EBITDA per employee in our Advertising business" in FQ4'24, compared to $1.5M in FQ3'24, with this strategic internal performance metric likely to drive operational efficiency along with bottom-line expansion moving forward.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons are likely why the consensus has already raised their forward estimates, with APP expected to generate an accelerating top/bottom-line growth profile at a CAGR of +20.7%/+30.7% through FY2027.

This is compared to the original estimates of +10.5%/+22.2%, while building upon the 3Y historical trend at +19%/+59.8%, respectively, with it underscoring the ad-tech company's renewed growth opportunities through the e-commerce advertising market.

APP Valuations

These reasons may also be why the market has upgraded APP's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 51.66x, up drastically from the 5Y mean of 29.76x and the sector median of 25.24x.

Assuming that the ad-tech company is able to deliver on the massively upgraded forward estimates, it seems that the stock is finally trading reasonably at FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.30x as well, despite the notable upgrade from the former's 3Y mean of 0.29x.

The same conclusion may be derived after comparing to APP's advertising/ad-tech peers, including Meta (META) at 1.62x, Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) at 1.24x, Digital Turbine, Inc. (APPS) at 2.51x, and The Trade Desk (TTD) at 2.52, aside from Perion Network (PERI) at 0.35x, with it implying the former's profitable high growth trend ahead.

So, Is APP Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

APP 3Y Stock Price

For now, APP has reached new heights of the $470s ranges as it continues to run away from the 50/100/200 day moving averages, with it underscoring the market's insatiable appetite for high growth AI SaaS success stories.

Much of the tailwinds are naturally attributed to the company's ability to deliver on outsized expectations, despite the consistently raised forward estimates.

This reason is also why APP's stock price gap continues to widen to our prior fair value estimates of $125.20 offered in our last article, based on the FQ3'24 annualized GAAP EPS of $5.00 (+40.4% QoQ/+316.6% YoY) and the FWD P/E GAAP valuation of 25.05x then (nearer to its sector peers).

Based on the raised FY2027 GAAP EPS estimates from $6.11 to $12.71, it goes without saying that the stock has also run away from our updated long-term price target of $318.40.

APP's embedded premium valuations and baked in upside potential are also why we would like to advise caution, however.

For reference, APP's ad-tech peer, TTD has had an extremely painful -32.9% one-day correction, attributed to the management's "series of small execution missteps," the subsequent FQ4'24 top-line miss, and the underwhelming FY2025 guidance.

This development naturally highlights the fact that a stock's premium valuations/outsized price performance come with high expectations, one that may be a double edged knife once those expectations are not met.

At the same time, while APP may have had early successes in the ecommerce advertising segment, it remains to be seen how things may develop in the near term, since the company has yet to fully develop its advertising system and "lack the full self-service capabilities needed to handle growth at scale."

This is on top of the fact that the advertising business is inherently lumpy as some of its competitors guide tougher YoY comparison in FY2025 attributed to the lack of political ad spending.

This is also why we are likely to see APP report a lumpy FY2025 performance, worsened by the potentially ramped up operating expenses from the ecommerce advertising development and the divestiture of its legacy Apps business.

As a result of the numerous moving parts and the immense baked in premium, we prefer to maintain our Hold rating here. Otherwise, investors whom got in early during our two Buy ratings in May 2024 and September 2024 should simply enjoy this winner's ongoing run up.

data.title: Cousins Properties Isn't Cheap Enough
data.description:
Way back in early August of 2021, one company that I decided to dig into was Cousins Properties (CUZ). For those not familiar with the business, it operates as a REIT that owns and leases out office properties. Coming out of the COVID-19 pandemic, this was an interesting space. A change to remote work during the pandemic and the potential for a return to work made this an intriguing space for potential upside. Unfortunately, I concluded at the time that shares of the business were looking rather pricey. I did acknowledge that the long-term outlook for shareholders was probably positive from an operational standpoint. But little did I know at that time that the office space industry would face so many challenges.

Fundamentally speaking, Cousins Properties has actually been doing quite well considering what all has been going on in this space. Despite owning assets in major cities that have experienced rising vacancy rates, the firm continues to boast growing occupancy rates. Rent per square foot is climbing, and its cash flow metrics are also growing. Unfortunately, the stock is still not the cheapest. However, it is getting close to the point of justifying an upgrade. I'm not going to go that far at this moment. But if the stock drops, or we see continued improvement from an operational standpoint, an upgrade could very well be in the cards.

Taking a look at Cousins Properties

Based out of Georgia, Cousins Properties operates as a REIT that develops, purchases, leases, and manages, office properties. But these aren't just any office properties and they aren't in random places or places of insignificance. The firm prioritizes what is called Class A office assets, which are essentially the highest grade of commercial properties located in prime areas. Conceptually, these would be the types of properties that would be in the highest demand, but that would also cost the most money. Even though this is an expensive place to be in, the end result, as time goes on, can be quite lucrative. Even as vacancy rates climb, these types of assets should fare better than your lower-class properties.

In addition to having this emphasis, the firm also focuses on acquiring properties in some of the largest cities in the Sun Belt. This is the southern part of the US that includes places like Florida and Texas. For quite some time now, these areas of the country have seen pretty sizable growth, seemingly because the aging population of the US prefers the warmer weather there. But success begets success and this leads to favorable economic conditions that draw even more people in. Obviously, lower taxes and a favorable cost of living compared to some other parts of the country, have also contributed to the boom in that area.

To put this all in perspective, today, the Sun Belt states account for over 50% of the total population of the US. By 2040, that number is expected to climb to 55%. And interestingly, over the last 10 years, the area has been responsible for roughly 80% of all of the population growth that the US has experienced. When it comes to Cousins Properties specifically, the company's biggest emphasis involves Atlanta, Georgia. Last year, 35.7% of the firm's profits came from that city. Not far behind, at 32.4%, was Austin, Texas. These are the two biggest areas for the business. However, it does also generate revenue from Charlotte, North Carolina; Tampa, Florida; Phoenix, Arizona; and both Houston and Dallas in Texas.

Even though vacancy rates have been a problem when it comes to office assets over the last few years, management has done a fine job growing the business. From 2022 through 2024, revenue for the company grew from $762.3 million to $856.8 million. This growth was driven by a few key factors. For starters, thanks to acquisitions and development projects that the company has completed, overall square footage has increased from 19.14 million to 21.11 million. In addition to seeing the overall size of its portfolio grow, the company has also seen an increase in its occupancy rate. Back in 2022, this number was 87.3%. But by the end of 2024, it had grown to 88.6%.

Another thing to take into consideration here that I view as a positive for shareholders is that leasing activity has been quite nice. In fact, the total contractual rent per square foot of properties that have either been with new tenants, that have been renewed by existing tenants, or that were entitled to increased rent because of expansion projects that the company invested in, rose over this window of time from $23.39 per square foot per year to $28.17 per square foot per year. New properties have seen this number climb from $24.24 per square foot to $30.50 per square foot, while renewals have seen a rise from $23.68 per square foot to $26.16 per square foot. It's nice to see these increases, especially with the most recent data showing that the lease term that the company is currently locking down is a weighted average of 7.9 years. That is down from the 9.2 years reported two years earlier. But it is up from the 7.5 years seen back in 2023.

Overall profitability for the company has been interesting. Actual net profits have declined from $166.8 million to $46 million over the last three years. However, net income really is not that valuable a metric in this space. Other cash flow figures have been generally positive. The one exception to this has been FAD, or funds available for distribution. Due to things such as capital expenditures, we have actually seen a slight decline from $274.5 million to $260.9 million over the last three fiscal years. However, operating cash flow has grown from $365.2 million to $400.2 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get an increase from $372.6 million to $422.3 million. FFO, or funds from operations, barely budged, rising from $408.8 million to $413.1 million. However, EBITDA for the company managed to rise from $484.5 million to $541.5 million.

Management continues to make investments in different growth initiatives. As an example, in December of last year, the firm announced the acquisition of Sail Tower, which is a major office property in Austin, Texas. This particular asset is roughly 804,000 square feet. It's quite new, having been built back in 2022. It boasts a 13-year weighted average lease term and is fully leased and occupied. The office portion of this asset is actually 100% leased to a Fortune 20 company through the year 2038. So absent something catastrophic happening, it will generate consistent and reliable cash flows for Cousins Properties for quite some time. To put in perspective how much such a major asset would cost, management ended up doling out $521.8 million for it. However, they did say that the 35-story building is ‘well below’ replacement cost and that rents that are currently in place in it are ‘significantly below’ what the market justifies. This could lead to even stronger returns as time goes on.

These kinds of investments have management confident that FFO this year will be between $460.5 million and $477.3 million. At the midpoint, that would represent an increase of 13.2% compared to the $414.1 million generated last year. If we assume that other major profitability metrics for the company also rise at the same rate, we would expect adjusted operating cash flow of $478.2 million, FAD of $295.4 million, and EBITDA of $613.2 million. In the chart above, you can see what this means for the valuation of the company on a forward basis. The chart also values the company using historical results for 2024. For a high quality REIT, these multiples are not bad.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Cousins Properties 12.1 15.2
SL Green Realty Corp. (SLG) 37.5 23.1
Kilroy Realty Corporation (KRC) 7.9 12.4
Douglas Emmett (DEI) 7.2 15.4
COPT Defense Properties (CDP) 11.7 16.5
Highwoods Properties (HIW) 7.7 10.8

For context, in the table above, I compared the company to five similar firms. It is true that, on a price to operating cash flow basis, four of the five companies I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is. But when we use the EV to EBITDA approach, we can see that this number drops to two of the five. Speaking of comparative analysis, in the chart below, I looked at the net leverage ratio of the company compared to the same five businesses. What I found was that its overall leverage is comfortably below any of these other firms. This means that Cousins Properties has wiggle room to grow even further if it so desires. However, even if it doesn't choose to take on additional leverage, its comparative standing should justify at least a slight premium compared to the competition.

This is not to say that there aren't risks associated with the business. Using third party data, I decided to look at vacancy trends in the cities in which the business operates. From 2023 through 2024, the vacancy rate in six of the seven cities that Cousins Properties owns assets in increased. This included both Atlanta and Austin. In the table below, you can see what I mean. Only Houston, which accounts for only 3.8% of the company's profits, saw an improvement. But again, this is for all office assets, not just Class A assets. If we did a weighted average based on profits for these cities, Cousins Properties would have a vacancy rate of 21.8%. However, as I mentioned already, its overall occupancy rate is 88.6%, while its leased rate is 91.6%. So there exists an 11.4% and 13.4% differential, respectively, compared to what the market is seeing. Add on top of this the aforementioned increases in rent per square foot, and I wouldn't be worried just yet.

Takeaway

All things considered, Cousins Properties strikes me as an interesting prospect. However, it's not one that I am terribly bullish about. The company certainly seems to be high quality and management is focused on growth. Its balance sheet leaves open the opportunity for additional rapid expansion relative to similar firms. All of this impressive performance comes in spite of the fact that the company has office assets located in cities that have seen a worsening in terms of their vacancy rates over the last year. But because of the quality of the assets, demand is still high. If the stock was cheaper, I would probably turn bullish. But for now, I think that keeping the firm rated a ‘hold’ makes the most sense.

data.title: 3 Areas I Am Under-Weight For A Trump Presidency
data.description:
Main Thesis/Background

The purpose of this article is to discuss the state of the broader market and highlight three areas I plan on not getting aggressive on in 2025. This is a different twist from most articles I write (and most you will see on the web as a whole) where authors discuss good investment opportunities. Contrastingly, this time around I am going to focus on some of the headwinds facing the market specifically due to the Trump administration, that extends to a few areas I am not bullish on.

I think this is important because one can be a "market bull" as I am long-term, but not necessarily finding value everywhere. A key to reaching true alpha is not just picking big winners, but also avoiding losers, and I see areas such as Energy, Emerging Market equities and debt, and Consumer retail as being potential losers in the year ahead. In this piece, I will touch on each and justify why I won't be a buyer of these areas going forward, unless there is a dramatic sell-off or some other fundamental shift in the market.

Sector #1: Energy

Focus number one is the Energy sector. The primary focus for me is large-cap US and European energy players, I don't dive too deeply into the smaller-cap realm of this sector (in this review and in general). While the sector has had a decent start to 2025 thus far, it is noticeable that earnings performance has been quite poor in relative terms against the other sectors in the market:

While weaknesses such as this can often represent a buyer or contrarian opportunity, this time around, I'm not so sure if that is the case. And there are two primary reasons I think this way. One, if we look back at history, we see that the Energy sector in fact didn't perform too well under President Trump. While the majority of the market - and all other major sector - saw substantial gains, the Energy sector was a big outlier:

Of course, past performance doesn't guarantee any future result. The Energy sector performed poorly back then, it is true, but the pandemic did have something to do with that!

However, it speaks to the broader thesis that I generally go under-weight this area when Republicans are in control of DC - as they are now. This is partially a Trump rationale, but really has to do with the shift of governing power from Democrats to Republicans as a whole. While the Republican Party is seen as being "energy friendly" - that is primarily due to the outlook in regulatory terms.

While a friendly regulatory environment can indeed be "good" (depending on your perspective) when it comes to equity performance in the Energy sector, it is a mixed bag. The reason being that if production is encouraged and more supply becomes available, price can decline. That can lead to lower equity prices despite a government that is favorable towards the sector.

And the key is we have already seen action by President Trump in this regard. One of his first acts in office was to declare a national "energy emergency" - highlighting the pressing importance of this issue and his commitment to it:

I see this as an expectation that supply is likely to rise in the US for the year ahead - and probably beyond that. This compounds on what was likely to be a year of surplus oil anyway - with the consensus being not if there would be a surplus, but by how much:

The premise here is the Energy market is likely to see some volatility ahead if supply does rise and the US government continues to encourage more production. While the recent uptick is a reflection of how Energy can perform well (through rising commodity prices) when there are economic or trade disruptions or geopolitical risks, my longer-term view is this sector will be an under-perform during Trump's first few years. This means I will be under-weight this sector going forward and would advise caution to my followers.

**I own the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLE) and the iShares Global Energy ETF (IXC) for exposure to the European majors. I will continue to hold these positions, but will only add if I see a significant correction or a shift in the tenor out of DC.

Sector #2: Emerging Markets (Equities & Debt)

The second source of uncertainty for me is the emerging market landscape. This includes both equities and debt markets - both of which I will be avoiding in the year ahead. I have written about the equity portion at length in other reviews (see here for the most recent) so I don't want to rehash too much of what I have previously written. I will touch on the equity markets briefly, but for this article I will focus primarily on the debt space.

On the surface, this is actually a spot where investors may be seeing some value. Yields are currently quite high - especially with inflation having moderated. When priced in either hard currency (such as the USD or Euro) and in local currency, yields are likely high enough across the board to pique some interest:

However, I do not view this as a buyer opportunity because I see comparable yields in US corporates and tax-exempt US municipal debt. So I have to have a compelling reason to shift my allocation to EM debt - and the backdrop to me is to avoid this sector, not bet on it.

One of the reasons for this is because I see 2025 as being a year of strength for the USD. When the dollar rises against EM currencies, that can have a more profound impact than it will have against more developed and stable economies (such as Canada or Western Europe). The fact is that EM debt is more volatile and a rising USD will pressure those local governments who must convert their currencies into USD if the bonds are issued in dollars. This puts pressure on the budgets and discretionary spending plans of those countries and puts economic growth at risk - a negative spiral.

But I must also answer why I believe the USD is going to be strong this year. After all, the Fed began a rate cutting cycle, so shouldn't the USD fall as interest rates go lower?

Therein lies the rub, and to tie this back to the Trump administration, I see impending tariffs and general trade uncertainty as catalysts for the Fed to stand pat. That means a more hawkish Fed in 2025 than previously expected (or hoped for) and should contribute to a bullish case for the USD in the year ahead. When we consider how inflation could be impacted by tariffs against a number of top US trading partners, this outlook should be clear:

While that may seem like a negligible impact on the US - keep in mind that inflation is already elevated (despite coming down over the past year). It remains above the Fed's prior 2% target, and an increase in inflation by any measure is likely to stay in the Fed's hand. To me, that means the Fed is going to remain on hold for the foreseeable future and that will keep the USD elevated, especially compared to where markets anticipated it trading in the year ahead.

The net impact to me is to remain long the USD through mostly US-oriented companies and to minimize exposure to emerging markets. This includes both debt and equity EM markets, and I will therefore keep my "avoid" rating in place for both of those ideas for now.

Sector #3: Retail/Consumer Discretionary

The final sector I'd go light on this year is retail - specifically consumer discretionary plays. This is an expansion on the tariff/inflation discussion above. Emerging markets are not the only potential losers in this scenario, consumers could be as well. While we might see some front-run purchasing in the US (and elsewhere) to get ahead of these tariffs, I would expect this uncertainty to cause some US households to pull back their purse strings a bit. This will lead to some retailers and manufacturers trying to absorb more of the costs to bring in buyers, hurting profits in the interim.

The conclusion I draw here is that if prices rise, those on moderate of fixed incomes are going to have to cut back. And even higher-income folks may be obliged to wait for the items they don't really need. That is where the caution on discretionary sectors comes into play - why buy now if you can wait and hopefully ride out the tariffs? Makes sense to me.

And this isn't just my thought. Looking at consumer sentiment metrics shows that many in the US are getting anxious about the prospect of tariffs and their impact on purchasing power. The "inflation expectations" index, curated by the University of Michigan, has soared recently."

The takeaway here is that consumers expect prices to price, and in a big way. And we have to remember that Americans are net importers of goods. So the impact of tariffs on this country in particular is going to be disproportionately higher than it may otherwise be in nations that are more self-reliant for their consumer products:

The point I am trying to convey here is that if prices go up, something has got to give. And that "give" is probably US consumers cutting back on imports and discretionary goods. While tariffs may be fleeting, the uncertainty they cause is not, and that makes me cautious about this sector near term.

Bottom-Line

The new year ushered in a new administration in Washington, with President Trump at the helm. He is already shaking things up, and that presents both opportunities and challenges. I see potential headaches in a few key areas, for equity and debt investors. In particular, I am going to be underweight Energy, emerging market debt and equities, and the retail/discretionary sector. While I am optimistic about the year ahead, I think selectivity will be key, and I hope my followers can use this as a guide on how to approach what is hopefully a profitable 2025.

data.title: The Macro Case For REITs Is Stronger Than You Think
data.description:
Co-produced by Austin Rogers.

Real estate property values and real estate investment trusts (“REITs”) have diverged over the last year.

According to the Green Street Commercial Property Price Index, overall CRE property values have rebounded ~5% off their lows hit in January 2024. And yet, REITs as measured by the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) are barely up 1% over the last year.

Why?

Because in recent years, the correlation between REITs and bond yields, especially the 10-year Treasury rate (US10Y), has been extremely tight. According to Mr. Market, REITs are no more than bond proxies.

Here's the relationship between the 10-year Treasury rate and the REIT index dividend yield over the last three years:

To be fair, since September 2024, the correlation between the 10-year and REIT yields has been less strong.

Why is this?

We think it's because of the sharp increase in the term premium.

As explained well by Russell Investments, Treasury yields are determined largely by two factors:

  1. Expectations of the Fed Funds Rate over the term of the bond
  2. A risk premium (i.e., the term premium) for longer duration bonds due to uncertainty about the future path of the Fed Funds Rate

The blue shaded areas in the chart above represent the change in expectations of the future Fed Funds Rate trajectory. The increase since the end of September 2024 represents the market's view that the Federal Reserve will execute fewer future rate cuts than previously believed.

The yellow shaded areas represent term premium.

What changed in September to cause this increase in the term premium?

In short, the catalyst was the rise in Trump's odds of winning the US presidency. According to polling aggregators like FiveThirtyEight, Trump's odds of winning began materially rising in late September. RealClearPolitics doesn't show the rise until mid-October because of a difference in poll inclusion criteria, but PredictIt, Polymarket, and other betting sites show Trump's odds of winning beginning to rally in late September.

The consensus economic view is that Trump's policy platform, including tax cuts, deregulation, tariffs, and deportations, will be simultaneously pro-growth and inflationary.

Just how inflationary, exactly?

No one knows. Hence, the term premium, which is an extra buffer built into Treasury yields to account for the uncertainty.

Here's an example of the range of possibilities being projected.

In December, the Congressional Budget Office estimated the impact of 10% universal tariffs and 50% tariffs on all Chinese imports to be an additional 1% added to inflation over the course of two years. (This does not include hypothetical retaliatory tariffs.)

On the other hand, the Peterson Institute for International Economics estimates that the full sweep of Trump's policies, if implemented as stated, would result in an increase in inflation between 4% and 7%.

Like the CBO, the PIIE estimates that the inflationary effect of Trump's policies would be mostly felt in the first two years. However, they also assert that inflation would remain sustainably 2 percentage points higher than it otherwise would be for as long as these policies remain in place.

Obviously, the scenario outlined by the CBO includes fairly mild inflation, while the scenario outlined by the PIIE includes fairly high inflation.

Which scenario is more likely to manifest?

The market doesn't know, and the price of that uncertainty is a higher 10-year Treasury yield.

In fact, as of this writing, the market is pricing basically equal probability of a Fed rate hike in 2025 as it is a Fed rate cut.

The market went from assigning a virtually 0% chance of a Fed rate hike in the next 12 months to now a 35% chance.

That is an extraordinary shift in the span of four months!

We believe this run up in Treasury yields, while understandable due to policy uncertainty, is overdone.

Here's our reasoning:

  • We don't think tax cuts are generally inflationary, but most Republicans' legislative efforts are simply going toward extending existing tax rates. We definitely don't view this as inflationary.
  • We see no evidence that deregulation will be inflationary.
  • Significantly curtailed immigration and an increase in deportations likely will be inflationary, but it will take years for the effects to fully play out, and cyclical economic weakening could offset lost workers.
  • Tariffs almost certainly will be inflationary as well, as the International Trade Commission confirmed about the 2018–2019 tariffs. But there's a good chance that their effect on overall inflation could still be fairly muted.

On that last point, consider that the vast majority of the US economy (roughly 2/3rds) is in domestic services. Less than 10% of the total economy comes from imported goods and services.

We do believe tariffs will be highly inefficient, inflationary, and disruptive to supply chains. We aren't ignoring that.

At the same time, one has to acknowledge that most of the CPI basket (most consumer spending) is not exposed to imports.

Certain consumers, businesses, and industries will be heavily impacted, but the population as a whole will be only minimally impacted.

Finally, a vitally important yet underappreciated point is that the shelter component of the CPI lags real-time rental inflation by over a year and is still not finished building in the current stagnation in residential rent rates.

As you can see from this chart, real-time residential inflation peaked in early 2022 and dropped sharply beginning in mid-2022. The shelter component of the CPI, however, didn't peak until early 2023 and has been slowly declining since then.

Before the pandemic, these two metrics were tightly correlated to each other. Over time, we think they will gradually converge again. This necessarily means that shelter CPI will come down further, which will exert downward pressure on the headline and core inflation rates.

Already, if the CPI used real-time residential inflation measurements instead of its shelter component, consumer inflation would have been under 2% for over a year and a half.

Regardless of the outcome of Trump's policies in 2025, the gradual disinflation of the CPI's shelter component will exert significant downward pressure on the overall CPI.

Bottom Line

All of the above leads us to believe that the spike in long-term interest rates is overdone.

And since REITs trade like bond proxies, the >10% selloff in REITs since mid-September is likewise overdone.

But within the REIT index, there is a wide dispersion in valuation. Some REITs have been punished far more than others.

For example, the Dividend King Federal Realty Trust (FRT), boasting an over 50-year dividend growth streak, is currently being priced at about 15x cash from operations (“CFO”), which is a close proxy for funds from operations (“FFO”):

This is well below its past two-decade average of about 20x FFO.

Why is a stalwart like FRT, which owns high-quality retail and mixed-use centers in many of the nation's largest metro markets, so discounted?

We believe it is because FRT is being treated by the market as nothing more than a bond proxy.

And consider another REIT stalwart, the Dividend Aristocrat NNN REIT (NNN), which used to be called National Retail Properties. At about 11x FFO, NNN is a far cry from its pre-COVID valuation of ~18x.

Why is NNN trading at a recessionary valuation?

While the REIT does have some minor tenant issues that have so far caused very little revenue disruption, we think the primary reason is NNN's sensitivity to interest rates.

When the 10-year rises, NNN falls. So, in turn, when the 10-year falls, NNN should enjoy a very nice rally.

Lastly, consider another, newer stalwart to hit the scene: VICI Properties (VICI), owner of such famous casino resorts as Caesars Palace, the Venetian, and MGM Grand. Like its peers above, VICI basically trades like a bond proxy. Hence, the growing discount to its average valuation since September 2024:

VICI today looks very cheap, and its 6% dividend yield looks very appealing for investors searching for safe and inflation-beating income.

If we are right that the selloff in bonds is overdone, then the selloff in REITs is likewise overdone.

That sets up a fantastic buying opportunity for high-quality yet rate-sensitive REITs like the three above. And these are precisely the types of REITs we are buying now at High Yield Landlord.

data.title: BlackRock: Assessing A $30 Billion Year
data.description:
BlackRock (NYSE:BLK), the largest asset manager in the planet, needs no introduction. Last year was a very good year for the firm. With $641 Bln of net new flows bringing assets under management – or AUM – to a record $11.6 trillion.

Aside from winning the asset magnet game last year, BlackRock also took significant steps to improve its margins through M&A transactions aimed at scaling up its private markets business.

In their role as a fiduciary, the firm charges management fees which are tied to AUM. In the short run, this exposes revenue to volatility related to stock and bond prices in general. Over the long-term, as stock prices rise and bond markets grow, it becomes a lasting tailwind that is separate from any new dollars that are gathered along the way. This type of growth requires zero incremental capital, to boot.

Revenue has been diversified slowly, yet base fees - or fees tied to AUM - are still the main source. Ten years ago, these fees amounted to 83% of revenue. Last year, that proportion was down to 76%. The other 1/4 is comprised of technology revenues, performance fees, advisory, securities lending and distribution fees.

The following chart shows how revenue is being gradually diversified away from base fees, but base fees remain the leading source.

Beep, beep, the basis point game:

Fee pressure has been a consistent challenge for all traditional asset managers. The switch to passive investing has been a huge boon for BlackRock through its leading iShares ETF business. ETFs have done to mutual funds what the internal combustion engine did to horses.

At the same time, passive investors, now expecting to match – and never exceed – their relevant indexes have correctly focused on fees as the main potential detractor from this goal. Vanguard recently announced a fresh round of price cuts on its products, the fee fight is still on.

Despite many strategies like separating “core” from “precision” and "active" ETFs, adding alternatives, growing retail distribution, etc., the overall base fee for BlackRock came down from +20 bps a decade ago to around 15 bps the last five years.

Although 5 basis points sounds small, the reduction means effective pricing is 20% lower in a period where inflation rose over 33% in the aggregate. The Vanguard effect takes its toll.

Three amigos to the rescue

To buck the stagnating fee trend, BlackRock pressed the M&A button not once, not twice, but three times last year (Technically there was another non-material acquisition, a bolt-on to their separate managed account business).

The three acquisitions are all intended to accelerate the firm’s progress in growing its “private markets” offering. Away from plain vanilla stocks and bonds, this segment brings together private equity, infrastructure investing, private and direct lending.

This is not new territory for BlackRock, but the acquired assets will scale their operations significantly (more on that below). Their motivation is crystal clear, this is the land of higher fees.

How much higher? Last year BlackRock reported a modest 4% of its AUM in “alternatives”, yet earned 16% of their base fees from them. Since the base fee average is around 15 bps, the implied base fee rate for alternatives would be 60 bps.

The first transaction was announced in January: for $3 billion in cash and 12 million shares (then trading at $800), BlackRock acquired Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP). This was followed in June by the acquisition of Preqin, for $3.2 billion in cash. To close out the year, BlackRock nabbed HPS for 12.1 million shares.

At the average stock price for 2024, this is nearly $30 billion transferred to the sellers, not a slow year at BlackRock. The common thread amongst the three is their focus on markets away from the traditional publicly traded securities and into less liquid, longer-term and higher-fee earning products.

Out of the three, Preqin is the smaller acquisition. With revenues of just $240 million, their data service platform is expected to be bolted onto Aladdin. We shall initially see the impact through an increase in technology revenues.

More strategically, Preqin is expected to be the base of which private market indexes are introduced into the market – as a first step to creating liquid products to track them (from iShares).

This will need to happen at some point to justify the 13x revenue price. Until it does come about, Preqin will have no impact on base fee rates. The other two, however, will move the needle from day one.

GIP, which closed last year, has already brought in $100 billion in infrastructure client assets, tripling the size of BlackRock in the space. The model is essentially private equity, but focused on long-lived, critical assets like ports, airports, water and sewer, rail, data centers, etc.

These types of investments appeal to institutional investors like pension funds, endowments and sovereign wealth funds which have infinite investment horizons and no need for daily liquidity.

The skill set acquired, therefore, is one of fundraising, engagement with regulators and governments, and operating company management. As in private equity - of which infrastructure is a subset, the big money is earned upon successful exits. However, applicable recurring base fees will certainly exceed 15 bps and contribute right of the gate to improving the overall rate for BLK.

Third, we have HPS, which has not closed yet, but will bring into the mix $148 billion of private lending AUM. Again, tripling BlackRock’s size to approximately $220 billion. Here it is easier to glean the immediate impact, with estimated fees disclosed at $850 million. This implies a rate of 57 bps, right in line with the 60 bps average fee rate of Alternatives at BlackRock.

The $850 million in fees are related to client assets before any growth from BlackRock’s distribution and reach. Keep in mind, BlackRock already has direct access to assets under insurance mandates and OCIO (Outsourced chief investment officer) mandates, of which allocations were increasingly going into private markets anyway. These allocations will now remain “in-house” to the extent the assets go to HPS or GIP funds.

The fact that both GIP and HPS owners took in most or all compensation in stock is encouraging. These are some of the most sophisticated investors out there. They have lockups and contingent shares. As much as it would have benefited us existing shareholders – at least initially - they would not have taken overpriced shares. They viewed BlackRock stock as fairly valued at prices of up to $1,000 per share.

Paying with shares has the further advantage of keeping people around and working with the right incentives. In the case of GIP, their Founder and CEO Bayo Ogunlesi is now part of BlackRock’s board of directors, highlighting the strategic importance of infrastructure investing going forward, and the importance of retaining the rainmakers.

Valuation gap

As a traditional asset manager, BlackRock's valuation has remained muted.

At around 20x, essentially all of the stock market performance has been driven by increased earnings from increased AUM - and share repurchases. In other words, the multiple has not helped. That may change in the coming years. The "private market" players, all of them except private credit firm Apollo, command PE ratios of over 40x.

The higher multiple attached to private market asset managers represents the better prospects for these businesses in terms of growth and sustainably higher fee structures. In addition, there is the prospect of expanding the menu for individual retirement accounts to include private funds. A potential tsunami of assets would then follow.

This is a touchy subject, I was skeptical until I heard some logical reasoning in a podcast featuring Mark Rowan (CEO of Apollo). He argued that private markets, with their illiquidity and long-term horizon are a better match for retirement accounts than publicly traded securities; and that daily liquidity is useless or worse for these accounts. Something to think about, but in any case, it is a possibility and serious lobbying is surely taking place.

What can go wrong?

With regard to the acquisitions, integration is always a tricky thing. However, BlackRock managed the mother of all integrations, merging different businesses and cultures with iShares after the GFC, earning a black belt in the process. Integrating these three should be manageable for them, one is a (large) bolt-on and the other two are double the size of existing operations.

With regard to BlackRock itself, the risk profile is tied to its size. As the largest asset manager, they get a lot of heat regarding passive investing and its influence (or lack of influence) on corporate governance.

They do have some programs to allow passive investors to communicate their voting preferences, however the logistics of implementing this mean they are limited mostly to institutional participants. It is a start in the right direction. In their fiduciary role, they should work to understand to the greatest extent possible the wishes of the asset owners – and then reflect these when voting on their behalf.

Conclusion

The busy M&A year reveals a sensible strategy. The three transactions taken together move BlackRock closer to being able to compete with others in private markets. Scale is important, as is reputation from track record, and they paid up for both.

As they grow these businesses organically, and through internal allocations of managed portfolios, we shall see base fee rates improve, and then the multiple should follow.

data.title: Our Top 10 Dividend High-Growth Stocks - February 2025
data.description:
Note: Please note that the stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article may be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. Such sections would be displayed in “italics,” and regular readers could skip them. However, we recommend that first-time readers refer to our BLOGPOST here to get a detailed explanation of our selection methodology/process and additional criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks.

Introduction:

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our proprietary and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P500 pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hypergrowth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks. The majority of our selections (if not all) have a market cap of $10 billion or more.

Who should follow this series?

The stocks highlighted in this series are for investors who are in their accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital at a rapid pace rather than their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they are not suitable for highly conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Market Outlook:

The bull market that started in Oct. 2022 is now 28 months old and is showing signs of some struggles amid a host of new worries. Eventually, the market is likely to climb the wall of worry, but for now, there are plenty of uncertainties. First, surely the market is just plain expensive and needs time to absorb the recent advances, especially in the tech sector. This was reminded when a number of high-flying technology and AI-related stocks nosedived in late January due to a panic reaction to China-based DeepSeek's announcement of much cheaper AI-language models (similar to ChatGPT). However, the market has recovered somewhat from the initial reaction. But, we should note that many blue-chip dividend-paying stocks have been performing a bit better than the tech names.

Second, we have the new US administration that comes with many positives for the market but also brings some uncertainties. The market remains uneasy and uncertain about President Trump's tariff regime.

With inflation still above the Fed's target, the market is only expecting two rate cuts in all of 2025. Currently, the CME FedWatch Tool puts the odds somewhat favorable for a rate cut in Jun. 2025. The relatively high interest rates are still causing problems in many sectors of the economy; for example, mortgage rates still remain elevated.

All in all, the economy is still on a sound footing, and the chances of a recession are very low. The interest rates are likely to come down, but at a much slower pace going forward. However, job growth is a cause for concern as growth in areas like technology, manufacturing, and professional services has flat lined. Many jobs in 2024 were created by the Govt. rather than the private sector. Still, on the positive side, some promised tax policies and looser regulations by the Trump administration may bolster the economic and M&A activities. As for the stock market, it is looking for a much-needed pause, which may mean some limited decline, before it climbs over the new wall of worries.

We can never be sure of the future direction of the markets. However, we intend to stay invested in stocks that have the best chance of growing rapidly in a bull market and not losing as much in a down market. The purpose of this series of articles is to highlight an outstanding list of high-growth stocks that also happen to have positive momentum behind them.

For more details and background information on this series, please read our blog post (the link provided at the beginning of the article).

Selection Criteria for Dividend Stocks:

This section briefly describes our selection criteria and process. For detailed information, please refer to the blog post (link provided earlier). We define our basic selection criteria here to bring our target list to roughly 400 stocks. Based on these criteria, we also calculate a “Dividend Safety Quality Score.” In brief, the Quality Score is derived using the following factors:

  • Current Yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth):
  • Payout ratio - Preferably based on Free Cash Flow.
  • Past five-year and 10-year dividend growth
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-yr dividend growth rate and the current yield
  • EPS growth (average of previous five years of growth and expected next 3–5 years' growth)
  • Debt/equity and Debt/asset ratios
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings)
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-wk high price)
  • Sales or Revenue growth for the past five years.

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks

We will now use the following additional criteria to filter out stocks that would fit the mold of high-growth DGI stocks.

  • The payment ratio (on a cash-flow basis or EPS basis) is less than 95% but preferably less than 80%.
  • 5-year annualized dividend growth (or 10-year growth rate) is at least 7.5% or greater. However, some exceptions can be made.
  • Chowder number (5-year dividend growth rate plus the current dividend yield) >= 9.

Until this stage of filtering, we kept the base criteria loose enough to allow a wide variety of stocks to make the list. However, we will perform additional filtering in the next stage to get the best candidates.

Most growth investors appreciate how important the earnings growth (earnings per share — EPS) is for a stock to grow its dividend rapidly. Without growth in earnings, the company cannot grow its dividends sustainably for long. So, our focus ought to be on earnings growth.

In our spreadsheet, we will add four more columns of data for each of the stocks:

  • EPS (earnings per share) Quality Rating
  • Last Qtr EPS change % (actual)
  • Current Qtr EPS change % (est.)
  • Current year EPS change % (est.).

We will now assign weights to these four sets of data for each stock and add them to the original “Dividend Safety Quality Score” to come up with a modified Quality Score tilted in favor of high dividend growth stocks. We will call this column “High-Growth Quality Score.”

Narrowing Down the List To 20 Stocks

The criteria listed above provide us with a list of roughly 350–400 stocks to work with. We will now filter it down and select roughly 50 stocks based on the following methodology.

  • We first select the top 20 stocks based on the highest High Growth-Quality-Score (adjusted for sector or industry over concentration). Please see Table-1A below.
  • We then take the top 10 stocks based on the highest 5-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1B below.
  • We will also select the top 10 stocks based on the highest 10-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1C below.
  • Finally, we will select the top 10 stocks based on average EPS Rating (earnings per share) and RS rating (Relative Strength). (EPS and RS ratings are taken from IBD; subscription is required). Please see Table-1D below.

Table-1A: Top 20 Highest HG-Quality-Score Stocks:

Table-1B: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (5 years):

Table-1C: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (10 years):

Table-1D: Top 10 Stocks with Highest EPS and RS Rating:

We will now combine these 50 stocks from the above four tables and remove any of the duplicates from this combined list, as some stocks may have qualified based on multiple criteria.

The following stocks appeared twice: AVGO, BK, CI, DFS, FIX, and SF (6 duplicates).

Appeared three times: MS (2 duplicates).

We are now left with 42 (50 - 8) names.

We will also remove any names where the revenue growth (over the past five years) has been negative. There is none on the list.

We will now calculate the average of EPS and RS (relative strength) ratings and remove any names that have an average of less than 60. This removes MOS, LEN, CI, ODFL, and OMF (leaving us 37 names).

We will also remove any names with both the EPS and RS less than 80. This removes CTRA, leaving us with 36 names.

Next, we will remove any stock that has an RS rating of less than 50. This removes SBAC, AMH, OVV, GWW, LRCX, and QCOM, leaving us with 30 names.

Finally, we will remove some stocks to avoid over-concentration from one sector or industry segment. During this process, we prioritize the stocks that have higher Relative Strength and EPS ratings.

Finance-Bank-major: We keep BK but remove BAC and SNV

Finance-Insurance: We keep PRI BRO but remove BRO.

Finance-All others: We keep DFS, COF, and MS but remove SF, GS, ARES, and V.

Computer and Business Software: We will keep SNX and SSNC and remove MSCI.

Electronics: We will keep KLAC and remove APH.

Industrial- Const. & Eng.:

Gold: We will keep RGLD but remove NEM.

We also remove some of the stocks with the lowest relative strength in the list. So, we removed MSCI. We are now left with 20 names.

In all, we removed nine names, so we are finally left with 20 (28-8) names, which are presented in the table below:

(FIX), (DLB), (ALSN), (BK), (FOX), (SNX), (SSNC), (EME), (KLAC), (DFS), (COF), (MS), (HWM), (PRI), (RGLD), (WSM), (DKS), (AVGO), (CTAS), and (NRG).

Table 2: Top 20 High Growth DGI Stocks of the Month:

Final Step: Selection of Top 10 High Growth DGI Stocks

Until now, we have mostly used our rule-based filtering criteria to come to a list of 20 stocks. However, we carefully avoided the high concentration of any one sector or industry segment. Now, this final step of bringing down the number of selections to just ten stocks is mostly a subjective process based on our judgment. We try to keep the group as diverse as possible, representing many sectors and industry segments. We encourage readers to look at the wider selection of 20 stocks and see if they come up with anything different and appropriate for their goals. Nonetheless, we describe below how we go about selecting these ten stocks for the month.

We sort our list of 20 stocks on sector/industry and then on the decreasing order of high-growth quality score. We try to select one stock from each industry segment from top to bottom; however, at times, multiple stocks could appear from the same sector. We try to keep the best stock in each industry segment based on the quality score. We also keep in view that this is not a permanent buy-and-hold portfolio, so we want to give more weightage to stocks that are likely to do well on a short to medium-term basis. That's why we are also going to give priority to stocks with solid EPS ratings and high positive momentum (Relative strength).

It is important to know that many of these stocks may be overvalued due to the high momentum. But in this list, we do not give much weightage to how a stock is priced in terms of valuation. Generally, their yields tend to be lower side as well, in the range of 1% to 2%.

Here are our top 10 selections for this month.

Current Month Selections (January 2025):

(FIX), (ALSN), (FOX), (SSNC), (DFS), (MS), (HWM), (WSM), (AVGO), and (NRG).

Previous Month Selections:

(FIX), (ALSN), (EME), (SF), (DFS), (WSM), (DKS), (AVGO), (TSM), and (NRG).

Since our methodology is largely based on a filtering process, we would like to point out that once a stock has appeared on our list, it may repeat for at least a couple of months. On average, we see about 30% to 40% turnover from one month to another, but it can vary greatly.

Note: Please note that if a stock was in a previous month's list but is no longer selected in the current list, it does not mean the stock is no longer a good choice. If the stock still scores an HG-Quality score of >70, we think it could still be at least a “hold” (if not a “buy”). However, for new selections, we consider many more factors than just the HG-Quality score. The purpose here is to highlight the top candidates every month. Please conduct further research and due diligence before making buy or sell decisions.

Table 3: (High Growth DGI)

Past Performance

Now, let's see how our model portfolio of these ten high-growth stocks would have performed compared to the benchmark Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF or the S&P 500 (SP500) from the beginning of 2013 until Jan. 2025. Many of our selections did not have a history going back to 2008, so we are only providing the comparison from 2013. The assumption is that our model portfolio was invested equally in ten stocks and rebalanced every year. Please note that one of the stocks, Fox Corp. (FOX), was excluded from the back-test for part of the period (from 2013 to 2020) as history was not available for this period.

Please remember that this is a back-tested performance, not an actual one. It is almost certain that we would not have picked the same set of stocks 12 years ago. So, this is more of an academic exercise but does provide a glimpse of the potential of these high-growth stocks. Also, past performance is no guarantee of any kind for future returns.

Chart-1:

Performance Since Jan. 2023:

Since the inception of this series, we have provided the progress and performance of the series. We compare the performance of the portfolio with the benchmark fund, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG), and also with the S&P 500. We provide the cumulative figures from the start date of Jan. 2023 until the current month. Obviously, the performance will vary from month to month. Furthermore, the performance of benchmarks may not match exactly with a buy-and-hold portfolio, as we take the performance from a specific start date and end date every month to match our 10-HG portfolio.

Table-3A:

Concluding Remarks

We had a disappointing performance from EMCOR (EME) last month, and it is being dropped for February. Also, we are dropping Stifel Financial (SF), DICK'S Sporting Goods (DKS), and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) from our list for the next month. Broadcom (AVGO) from the semiconductor industry remains on our list. Williams-Sonoma (WSM) remains on the list, but DKS is being dropped after staying on the list for many months. Once again, Comfort Systems (FIX) and NRG Energy (NRG) continue making it to our top 10, as they have for several months now.

Please note that our list changes from month to month. However, investors who have a longer-term horizon could still keep a stock even if we may have dropped, as long as it is still scoring well in terms of quality score.

The stocks selected in this series, even though they are excellent choices, are still subject to market volatility. Since these are high-growth stocks, we should expect them to have high volatility as well, so due diligence is required.

data.title: Proven Investment Strategies And $49.2 Billion In AUM Make Bridge A Buy
data.description:
Bridge Investment Group Holdings Inc. (NYSE:BRDG) reports $49.2 billion of assets under management and access to proprietary data and technology platforms to find investment opportunities. Offering returns to investors for many years and a team of investment professionals, Bridge Investment will most likely obtain management fees and performance fees in the future. In addition, considering the recent decrease in the interest rates, I would be expecting net income growth from strategies related to debt and mortgage-backed securities. Besides, considering the company's ability to deliver net income and FCF, I think that making forecasts about the future FCF growth is easy. Under my different financial models, BRDG appears to be a buy. According to Seeking Alpha, the company trades at 2x TTM cash flow, 7x FWD EBITDA, and 11x Non-GAAP EPS.

Bridge Investment: $49.2 Billion, And A Smart Team

Founded in 2009, the company presents itself as an alternative investment manager with $49.2 billion of assets under management. With a team of investment professionals, Bridge appears to be an expert in developing investment strategies focused on the real estate, credit, and renewable energy among other markets.

Among all the strategies, I think that the most remarkable are the company's strategies around bridge agency mortgage-backed securities and debt. In addition, the company also reports $6.5 billion of AUM in the development investment program. In the last annual report, the company reported more than 2,300 people working for the organization. The number of strategies is not small. The company gave a lot of information in the corporate website.

The Bridge Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities and Debt Strategies platforms seek to create high and sustainable current income while carefully managing risk. We execute with a deep knowledge of our markets coupled with a detailed, on-the-ground due diligence process. Source: Company's Website - Strategies

Our development investment program is multifamily-focused, with over 80% of the equity from our Opportunity Zone funds invested in multifamily development. The Opportunity Zone funds within our Development platform have a longer tenor than our other closed-end funds due to the 10-year hold required by the QOZ rules. As of December 31, 2023, our Development platform had approximately $6.5 billion of AUM. Source: 10-K

It is worth noting that the company launched new strategies in 2021, 2022, and 2023. Considering those new programs I would be expecting new investors, and more assets under management in the coming years.

In 2021, we launched our Logistics platform, in 2022, we launched our SFR and renewable energy platforms, and in 2023 we launched our secondaries platform. We are also launching new product offerings within these strategies to expand our distribution efforts. Source: 10-k

The company noted that equity strategies generally pay investment management fees of between 1.0% and 2.0% and incentive fees of between 15% and 20% above a preferred return hurdle. The debt strategies include investment management fees of around 0.85% and 1.75% and incentive fees close to 15% above a preferred return hurdle.

The company reports multichannel distribution capabilities and connections with large global institutions and individual investors. Given the number of connections with investors, I believe that further positive results will most likely lead to more assets under management, balance sheet expansion, and free cash flow growth.

In my view, Bridge is unique versus other alternative investment funds because of the company's team. The executive chairman was Head of Global Investment Banking for Citigroup, and CEO of Citigroup’s Asia Institutional Clients Group from 2004-2008.

In addition, the CEO used to work for Wells Fargo Bank, and was responsible for managing over $8 billion in assets. He was also partner with The Pacific Group USA, Inc., and with Bridge Loan Capital Fund. Given the expertise accumulated in large banks, the team will most likely sign new beneficial deals in the future.

Besides, the CFO worked for Ernst & Young, LLP for 16 years, and has a lot of expertise overseeing SEC reporting. In my view, it is quite beneficial. It means that the CFO may know well how to connect well with the investment community. She will most likely be able to offer the information shareholders need to assess the company's business model.

She was responsible for overseeing the execution of audits, along with focusing on technical accounting, real estate valuations, internal controls and SEC reporting. Source: Katie Elsnab From The Company's Website

I also believe that the company's on-the-ground resources, and connections with local market teams will most likely offer a considerable number of opportunities. The company currently operates across 35 states.

Investment team collaboration combined with our on-the-ground resources and local market teams provides us with extensive and unique deal flow across our target markets. Source: 10-k

In the new quarterly report, the market expects EPS Normalized of $0.16, and quarterly revenue of $80 million. Given previous EPS results, and surprises I think that the company will most likely deliver better than expected EPS results.

The market also expects 19% EPS growth in 2025, 32% in 2026, and 16% in 2027. The company trades at close to 6x 2027 EPS. From here, Bridge Investment Group looks like a buy.

Further Interest In Investment Funds, And Absolute Return Funds Could Accelerate Net Income Growth

Bridge will most likely accelerate the amount of dollars made thanks to a number of ongoing asset management trends. I think that the growing importance of alternative investment strategies may help Bridge Investment. In addition, the growing demand for private markets from private wealth fund investors, bank consolidation, and increased regulatory requirements are other factors that may help Bridge receive new clients. According to market experts the global alternative investment funds market could grow at close to 7.9% CAGR from 2024 to 2032.

The global alternative investment funds market was valued at $12.8 trillion in 2023, and is projected to reach $25.8 trillion by 2032, growing at a CAGR of 7.9% from 2024 to 2032. Source: Allied Market Research

Access To Proprietary Data And Technology Platforms May Enhance The Company's Ability To Find New Opportunities

Given the total amount of assets under management, the company may be able to pay the development of large proprietary data and technology platforms that will most likely help asset managers. Thanks to this information, Bridge will most likely find more targets and suitable investments for its clients. As a result, I believe that Bridge could offer higher returns, and manage a larger amount of dollars than small asset management firms. Management provided some details about these matters in the last quarterly report.

Our proprietary data and technology platforms, analytical tools and deep industry knowledge allow us to provide our fund investors with customized investment solutions, including specialized asset management services, tailored reporting packages, customized performance benchmarks as well as experienced and responsive compliance, administration, and tax capabilities. Source: 10-Q

Current Business Environment Will Most Likely Benefit The Company's Business Model

The current business environment may benefit some of the company's investment strategies. The rate cuts that are expected in the coming quarters may accelerate the demand for credit from companies out there. In addition, many companies may also decide to refinance their debt obligations in 2025 and 2026. With the company's strategies around the credit markets, the company may find new opportunities.

During 2024, inflation continued to decelerate and moderated at approximately 2.5%, which led to the Federal Reserve making its first rate cut in four years during its September 2024 meeting. It is expected that the Federal Reserve will make additional rate cuts in coming quarters. Source: 10-Q

In addition, as soon as the interest rates decline, I would be expecting a reactivation of certain industries that may not be growing a lot in 2025. As a result, investors out there may decide to invest more dollars in 2025 and 2026. The total amount of assets under management reported by Bridge may increase, and the expected management fees too. Finally, it is also worth noting that Bridge makes money out of performance fees. If the economic outlook improves right after the decline in interest rates, Bridge may make more money thanks to performance fees.

Our weighted-average management fee varies by fund and is based upon the size of the commitment; however, the low average for a single fund is 0.50% and the high average for a single fund is 1.99% of committed or invested capital for our closed-end funds. Source: 10-Q

We earn two types of performance fee revenues: incentive fees and performance allocations, as described below. Incentive fees comprise fees earned from certain fund investor investment mandates for which we do not have a general partner interest in a fund. Performance allocations include the allocation of performance-based fees, commonly referred to as carried interest, from limited partners in the funds to us. Source: 10-Q

Cash Flow Statement Review

Given a number of assumptions about the future financial statement, readers may want to have a look at previous cash flow figures. In the last five years, the company reported net income larger than $140 million every year, with the exception of the year 2023.

In the past, cash flow from operations included $153 million, $130 million, $209 million, $228 million, and $175 million per year. The largest capital expenditures that I could see were close to $26 million. Considering these results, I think that running a DCF model appears easy. I believe that making forecasts about future free cash flow looks easy because the company's figures did not change that much in the most recent history.

Balance Sheet Review

The balance sheet indicates a substantial increase in the total amount of assets driven by increases in long-term investments, goodwill, and notes receivable.

The total amount of liabilities also increased, however they increased at a lower rate than the total amount of assets. The total amount of long-term debt increased substantially, but I would not expect most investors to be afraid of the company's total debt obligations. With a significant number of investment professionals inside Bridge Investment, I would expect that Bridge will most likely control pretty well risks with regard to the total amount of debt.

Challenges

I believe that large declines in the company's stock returns would most likely lead to a deterioration of the management fees and performance fees as well as free cash flow declines. A deterioration of the economic outlook would most likely lead to lower valuation of the investments made, and the company may also receive a lower amount of financing. Under certain case scenarios, I think that the total amount of assets under management may decline, which could lead to net income growth declines. In this regard, Bridge offered the following information.

Although our AUM and fees generated have grown significantly since our inception and particularly in recent years, a significant deterioration in the returns we generate for our fund investors, adverse market conditions or an outflow of capital in the alternative asset management industry in general, or in the real estate space in which we specialize, could negatively affect our future growth rate. Source: 10-Q

If Bridge fails to find new opportunities, or the number of investment opportunities decrease, I would be expecting lower performance fees and a decrease in the company's net income growth. Besides, according to the company's quarterly report, Bridge noted that an increase in the interest rates would lower the returns on debt and mortgage-backed securities, which may also deteriorate future financial results.

Capital deployed may vary significantly from period to period with the fluctuating availability of attractive opportunities, which are dependent on a number of factors, including debt financing, the general macroeconomic environment, market positioning, valuation, size, the liquidity of such investment opportunities, and the long-term nature of our investment strategies. Each of these factors impacts our ability to efficiently and effectively invest our pool of fund capital and maintain revenue growth over time. Increases in prevailing interest rates could affect not only our returns on debt and mortgage-backed securities, but also our ability to deploy capital for Bridge-sponsored funds due to the increased cost of, and ability to secure, borrowings. Source: 10-Q

Valuation Cases Using Previous Financial Results, Challenges, And My Own Assumptions

Considering previous financial results, previous FCF growth, and revenue growth, I designed the following financial model. I took into account that the global alternative investment funds market is expected to grow at close to 7.9% CAGR in the coming years. Hence, under my best-case scenario, I used revenue growth of 5% from 2025 to 2035. Besides, from 2019 to 2023, revenue increased from $246 million to $381 million. I think that my numbers are conservative.

My numbers also include FCF of $159 million in 2028, $116 million in 2034, and $109 million in 2035. Besides, 2035 revenue would stand at $669 million. I took a look at the company's FCF margin, and previous FCF to design my FCF forecasts. I also checked the FCF margin reported by Bridge, and peers in the past. Under my best-case scenario, the company's FCF margin ranges from 33% in 2028 to 16% in 2035.

With these figures, I also assumed cost of capital of 6.1% and a terminal FCF multiple of 9.7x. My cost of capital is not far from the WACC used by other financial advisors. In addition, the company traded at more than 10x FCF in the past. Putting everything together, the results of my financial model included an enterprise value of $1.752 billion. It is worth noting that I used minority interest of $470 million. Hence, the enterprise value minus the minority interest of $470 million, minus net debt of $401 million, and divided by 41.8 million class A shares, I obtained a target price of $21 per share.

My worst-case scenario included revenue growth of 1% from 2025 to 2035. As a result, 2035 revenue would be close to $420 million, with 2034 FCF of $76 million and 2035 FCF of $69 million. The FCF margin used ranges from 30% in 2029 to 16% in 2035. In this case, I used a WACC of 6.1% and a FCF multiple of 9.7x. Under the previous conditions, I obtained an enterprise value of $1.287 billion. If we also take into account the net debt, and the minority interest, and divide by 41.8 million class A shares, the fair price is close to $9.9 per share.

My Takeaway

Bridge Investment Group reports $49.2 billion of assets under management and proprietary data and technology platforms that will most likely accelerate the work of asset managers. Besides, with a team of investment professionals and know-how about alternative investment strategies, I would expect further net income growth. I assumed that after many years in industry, Bridge Investment Group will most likely continue to find investment opportunities. I also believe that the recent decrease in the interest rates could benefit the company's returns on debt and mortgage-backed securities. Putting everything together with DCF models, my results implied a fair valuation that is significantly higher than the current stock price. From here, the company looks like a buy.

data.title: Mesoblast: Ryoncil Approval Overshadowed By Commercial Hurdles
data.description:
Introduction

Australia-based Mesoblast (MESO) has skyrocketed 138% since my "sell" recommendation last June and 827% in the past year. This rally can be largely attributed to the FDA approval of Ryoncil (remestemcel-L) in December for the treatment of pediatric steroid-refractory acute graft-versus-host disease (SR-aGVHD). Recall that the mesenchymal stromal cell therapy was denied twice before. While Ryoncil's eventual approval did not totally surprise me, I'm still hesitant on its commercial prospects. As I stated in my last analysis:

After a third regulatory submission, they may find success in pediatric SR-aGVHD, but, as evidenced by my DCF analysis, this is a small and limited market.

Assuming 100% market share, I estimated that Ryoncil could generate as much as $250 million in peak annual revenue. However, due to the higher costs associated with manufacturing cell therapies, the net present value was just ~$200 million (prior to FDA approval). Its approval bumps the NPV to ~$400 million. In the meantime, Mesoblast's market capitalization has blossomed to $2.33 billion. So, what gives? The following article reassesses Ryoncil in light of its approval and Mesoblast's other notable prospects.

Ryoncil in SR-aGVHD: FDA Approval Opens Small But Complex Market

According to treatment recommendations (e.g., UpToDate), Ryoncil appears slated for second-line pediatric SR-aGVHD, behind ruxolitinib (off-label). Moreover, Ryoncil is not definitively preferred over other options after ruxolitinib, such as mycophenolate, sirolimus, and brentuximab vedotin. How preferred Ryoncil is will probably depend on how available it is and reimbursement. Mesoblast is tasked with scaling manufacturing of the complex cell therapy and convincing payers that its hefty price tag is worthwhile. This will not be easy for a small company that has no prior market experience. Still, I think Ryoncil warrants similar modeling to my last. Mesoblast has not yet revealed Ryoncil's pricing, but industry analysts expect it to be priced aggressively, between $500,000 and $1 million per patient. This is considerably higher than my previous estimate.

I think most of my assumptions are optimistic. For instance, an 80% market penetration suggests broad utilization in second-line SR-aGVHD. Mesoblast may price Ryoncil higher than $675,000, but then risks push back from payers. The final NPV is approximately $1 billion. I believe that the model above is an example of a successful commercialization in second-line SR-aGVHD. By the way, the model (1,000 patients) also assumes approval in adults (Phase 3). The pediatric population alone is likely much smaller than 1,000.

The company has partnered with Cencora for distribution and will target high-volume pediatric transplant centers.

Rexlemestrocel-L: Targeting the Crowded Chronic Back Pain Market

Outside of pediatric SR-aGVHD, Mesoblast is evaluating rexlemestrocel-L in patients with chronic low back pain due to inflammatory degenerative disc disease in a Phase 3 trial (NCT06325566). The trial will enroll ~300 patients. Rexlemestrocel-L will be administered as an intradiscal injection and compared to a saline solution. After 12 months, low back pain intensity will be evaluated via a visual analog scale score between 0 and 100. Adverse events will be monitored for up to 24 months.
